attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a five-day history of fever, cough, and progressive shortness of breath. He returned from Saudi Arabia one week ago where he had been working as a camel farm manager for three months. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 125/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 28/min, SpO2 89% on room air, and temperature 38.9°C. Chest examination reveals bilateral crackles. Chest X-ray shows bilateral patchy infiltrates.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with severe respiratory symptoms and has significant epidemiological risk factors for Middle East respiratory syndrome coronavirus (MERS-CoV) infection, namely recent travel to Saudi Arabia and close contact with camels. Middle East respiratory syndrome typically presents with fever, cough, and shortness of breath that can progress to severe respiratory failure. The incubation period is typically 5-6 days but can range from 2-14 days.

Q fever, caused by Coxiella burnetii, can be transmitted from livestock including camels, but typically presents with hepatitis and pneumonia. While possible, it would be less likely given the severity of respiratory symptoms.

Brucellosis, while also associated with exposure to camels and other livestock, typically presents with a more indolent course characterised by undulating fever, night sweats, and musculoskeletal symptoms rather than severe respiratory illness.

Tuberculosis would typically have a more chronic course with symptoms developing over weeks to months, often accompanied by weight loss and night sweats. While TB can be transmitted from camels, the acute presentation makes this less likely.

Legionnaires' disease typically occurs in the context of exposure to contaminated water systems rather than animal contact. While it can cause severe pneumonia, the epidemiological history strongly favours MERS-CoV in this case.

MERS-CoV has a high mortality rate of approximately 35%. Dromedary camels are the primary reservoir for MERS-CoV, and most human cases have been linked to direct or indirect contact with infected camels or camel products. Healthcare workers caring for MERS patients are also at risk of infection through human-to-human transmission.
learningPoints: Middle East respiratory syndrome coronavirus (MERS-CoV) is primarily transmitted to humans through direct or indirect contact with infected dromedary camels.
Recent travel history to the Middle East and exposure to camels are key risk factors for MERS-CoV infection.
MERS-CoV presents with severe respiratory symptoms and has a high mortality rate.
references: Memish ZA, Perlman S, Van Kerkhove MD, Zumla A. Middle East respiratory syndrome. Lancet. 2020;395(10229):1063-1077. doi:10.1016/S0140-6736(19)33221-0
